perturbation.original_text: Any royalty payment that is not paid when due will bear interest from the date such amount is due until the date payment is made at a rate equal to ten percent (10%) per annum.
perturbation.changed_text: Any royalty payment that is not paid when due will bear interest from the date such amount is due until the date payment is made at a rate equal to twenty-six percent (26%) per annum.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the interest rate for late royalty payments to 26% per annum can be considered usurious under Texas law, which limits the maximum interest rate that can be charged.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Finance Code § 302.002 sets the maximum interest rate at the prime rate plus 5%, which will rarely reach 26%. Charging a higher rate could violate usury laws. The perturbation changes the interest rate on late payments from 10% to 26% per annum, creating a potential violation of Texas usury laws if the agreed rate exceeds the legally allowed maximum.

perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the interest rate for late royalty payments from 10% to 26% per annum. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this could be usurious under Texas law, which limits the maximum interest rate that can be charged. The Law Explanation states that Texas Finance Code § 302.002 sets the maximum interest rate at the prime rate plus 5%, which will rarely reach 26%. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions Texas Finance Code Section 302.002. Therefore, the changed text creates a potential violation of Texas usury laws by exceeding the legally allowed maximum interest rate, constituting a legal contradiction as defined in the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the expiration or any termination of this Agreement, Licensor shall repay to Licensee the aggregate amount of all Royalty Advances then outstanding.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the expiration or any termination of this Agreement, Licensor shall repay to Licensee the aggregate amount of all Royalty Advances then outstanding within 180 days of termination.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the repayment of Royalty Advances to 180 days after termination may create an unreasonable delay in payment that could be viewed as a breach of contract and may violate Texas's prompt payment laws which mandate payments be made promptly.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Business and Commerce Code Section 28.004 relates to prompt payment for completed work. While Royalty Advances may not fall directly under the definition of 'completed work', imposing an unreasonable delay on their repayment can be considered a breach of the implicit covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original text requires repayment upon termination, while the modified text delays that repayment to 180 days after termination, creating a potentially unreasonable delay that may violate Texas's business regulations regarding prompt payment.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 180-day delay in repaying Royalty Advances, whereas the original text mandates repayment 'upon the expiration or any termination'. This delay, as highlighted in the Perturbation and Law explanations, potentially violates Texas's prompt payment laws and can be seen as a breach of the implicit covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Although there are no scraped law snippets to draw from, the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states the contract is contradictory if it 'sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.' Here, the introduction of the 180-day delay introduces a potential conflict with prompt payment principles, fulfilling the conditions for a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective through and including March 31, 2004. Upon the expiration of such term, this Agreement will renew automatically for successive terms of one (1) year each unless either party to this Agreement delivers written notice of termination to the other party to this Agreement at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the original or any renewal term.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective through and including March 31, 2004. Upon the expiration of such term, this Agreement will renew automatically for successive terms of one (1) year each unless either party to this Agreement delivers written notice of termination to the other party to this Agreement at least fifteen (15) days prior to the end of the original or any renewal term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 15 days may be deemed unreasonably short, potentially violating Texas's requirements for reasonable notice in contractual relationships, particularly if it causes undue hardship to the other party.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Business and Commerce Code Section 1.205 specifies what is reasonable time. By reducing the notice period from 60 days to 15 days, the modification potentially violates what is deemed reasonable. The original text provides a 60-day notice period before termination. The modified text reduces that period to 15 days. This significantly shorter period may be considered commercially unreasonable, especially given the financial implications and the time needed to wind down a distributorship, potentially violating Texas contract law requirements for reasonable notice and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: he reasonably predictable fair market rent for the use of the goods for the term of the renewal at the time the option is to be performed; or (6) the lessee has an option to become the owner of the goods for a fixed price that is equal to or greater than the reasonably predictable fair market value of the goods at the time the option is to be performed. (d) Additional consideration is nominal if it is less than the lessee's reasonably predictable cost of performing under the lease agreement if the option is not exercised. Additional consideration is not nominal if: (1) when the option to renew the lease is granted to the lessee, the rent is stated to be the fair market rent for the use of the goods for the term of the renewal determined at the time the option is to be performed; or (2) when the option to become the owner of the goods is granted to the lessee, the price is stated to be the fair market value of the goods determined at the time the option is to be performed. (e) The "remaining economic life of the goods" and "reasonably predictable" fair market rent, fair market value, or cost of performing under the lease agreement must be determined with reference to the facts and circumstances at the time the transaction is entered into. Amended by Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 542, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 2003. Sec. 1.204. VALUE. Except as otherwise provided in Chapters 3 , 4 , and 5 , a person gives value for rights if the person acquires them: (1) in return for a binding commitment to extend credit or for the extension of immediately available credit, whether or not drawn upon and whether or not a charge-back is provided for in the event of difficulties in collection; (2) as security for, or in total or partial satisfaction of, a preexisting claim; (3) by accepting delivery under a preexisting contract for purchase; or (4) in return for any consideration sufficient to support a simple contract. Amended by Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 542, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 2003. Sec. 1.205. REASONABLE TIME; SEASONABLENESS. (a) Whether a time for taking an action required by this title is reasonable depends on the nature, purpose, and circumstances of the action. (b) An action is taken seasonably if it is taken at or within the time agreed or, if no time is agreed, at or within a reasonable time. Amended by Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 542, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 2003. Sec. 1.206. PRESUMPTIONS. Whenever this title creates a "presumption" with respect to a fact, or provides that a fact is "presumed," the trier of fact must find the existence of the fact unless and until evidence is introduced that supports a finding of its nonexistence. Amended by Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 542, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 2003. SUBCHAPTER C. TERRITORIAL APPLICABILITY AND GENERAL RULES Sec. 1.301. TERRITORIAL APPLICATION OF THE TITLE; PARTIES' POWER TO CHOOSE APPLICABLE LAW. (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. Failing such agreement this title applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (b) Where one of the following provisions of this title specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law (including the conflict of laws rules) so specified: Rights of creditors against sold goods. Section 2.402 . Applicability of the chapter on Leases. Sections 2A.105 and 2A.106 . Applicability of the chapter on Bank Deposits and Collections. Section 4.102 . Governing law in the chapter on Funds Transfers. Section 4A.507 . Letters of Credit. Section 5.116 . Applicability of the chapter on Investment Securities. Section 8.110 . Law governing perfection, the effect of perfection or nonperfection, and the priority of security interests and agricultural liens.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for termination from 60 days to 15 days. The Law Explanation states this shorter period may be considered commercially unreasonable, potentially violating Texas contract law requirements for reasonable notice. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 1.205, states that whether a time for taking an action is reasonable depends on the nature, purpose, and circumstances of the action. The change from 60 to 15 days could be deemed unreasonable, creating a legal contradiction as the original reasonable time was reduced.
